heading: SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO CXIII
card_summary: This chunk concludes Campanini's analytical catalogue of Zorzi's Kabbalistic sources in the De Harmonia Mundi, identifying the final entries: El'azar da Worms's Sha'are ha-sod (§45), Isaac of Acre's Me'irat 'enayim as "Lumen oculorum" (§46), Abraham ben Alexander of Cologne's Keter Shem Tov as "Corona nominis boni" (§47), the Sefer ha-Temunah as "Sepher Temunot" (§48), Heikhalot literature as "Mercava Ezechielis" (§49), Menahem Recanati's Commentary on Prayers as "De secretis orationum" (§50), and Abraham Abulafia's Imre Shefer as "Imre sepher, id est eloquia pulchritudinis" (§51). The chunk also notes an uncatalogued citation of the "thirty-two paths" from the Sefer Yetzirah tradition, and closes with Campanini's synthetic assessment of Zorzi's originality within Renaissance Christian Kabbalah — positioning him as a thinker who, perhaps for the last time, envisioned a unified synthesis of Neoplatonism, Hermetism, and Hebrew Kabbalah before these currents diverged into the new science (Copernicus, Kepler, Newton), Bruno's speculations, and Kircher's esotericism.
essay:
# SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO CXIII (pp. 113–118): Final Kabbalistic Sources and Campanini's Synthetic Assessment

## Concluding the Catalogue of Kabbalistic Sources (§§45–51)

This chunk completes Campanini's exhaustive source-by-source analysis of the *catalogus* of Kabbalistic works cited in the *De Harmonia Mundi*, offering identifications for the final seven entries. The discussion is simultaneously a bibliographical detective exercise and an argument about the depth and originality of Zorzi's Hebrew learning.

### §45 — The Anonymous Pietist Work (El'azar da Worms)

Campanini revisits an entry discussed earlier, proposing as an alternative identification the work of the Rhineland Pietist **El'azar da Worms**, the *Sha'are ha-sod ha-yichud we-ha-emunah* ("Chapters on the Secret of Unity and Faith"). This text circulated anonymously in manuscript and played a significant role in the genesis of Kabbalistic literature. Crucially, it had been translated into Latin by **Flavio Mitridate** for **Giovanni Pico della Mirandola**, establishing the chain of transmission that connects Zorzi to the Pichian circle.

### §46 — *Lumen oculorum* (Isaac of Acre)

The title *Lumen oculorum* is identified with high probability as *Me'irat 'enayim* by **Isaac of Acre** (or Acco). However, Campanini notes the complete absence of explicit citations traceable to this work, suggesting Zorzi's acquaintance was only indirect.

### §47 — *Corona nominis boni* (Avraham ben Alexander of Cologne)

This entry is identified as the *Keter Shem Tov* of **Avraham ben Alexander of Cologne**, a text of major importance as a bridge between German Hasidic mysticism and Spanish Kabbalah. Like several other works in the catalogue, it was translated by Flavio Mitridate for Pico and appears under this identical Latin title in ms. **Vat. Ebr. 190** — a key manuscript for the history of Christian Kabbalah.

### §48 — *Sepher Temunot* (Sefer ha-Temunah)

Campanini argues that the *Sefer ha-Temunah* ("Book of the Figure") is one of the texts Zorzi consulted directly, as evidenced by at least one explicit internal reference in the *De Harmonia Mundi*. The work's plural form — "figures," meaning mystical interpretations of Hebrew letters — makes Zorzi's title *Temunot* (plural) understandable. Its importance for Christian Kabbalah is confirmed by its translation into Latin by **Felice da Prato** for **Cardinal Egidio da Viterbo**. Campanini also traces a Venetian connection: the *Sefer ha-Temunah* is likely among the Kabbalistic works promised for publication by **Daniel Bomberg** in the preface-letter to Avraham De Balmes's *Miqneh Avram* (*Peculium Abrae*):

> "il progetto di Bomberg, per qualche ragione, non ebbe seguito, ma l'annuncio editoriale acquista autonoma rilevanza come documento di un interesse e di una diffusione di idee e libri cabbalistici in ambienti cristiani."

<cite>p. 113–114</cite>

### §49 — *Mercava Ezechielis* (Heikhalot Literature)

This entry is tentatively linked to the *Heikhalot* literature (mystical "Throne/Chariot" texts), though no extant work bears this exact title. Alternatively, Campanini suggests Zorzi may have inferred such a work from the recurring formula *be-merkavat Yechezqel* ("in [the vision of] the Chariot of Ezekiel") found in the **Mosheh Botarel** commentary on the Sefer Yetzirah — a recurring intermediary source throughout this catalogue.

### §50 — *De secretis orationum* (Menahem Recanati)

Campanini identifies this title as the *Perush 'al ha-tefillot* (Commentary on Prayers) by **Menahem Recanati**, another major text in early Christian Kabbalah. Its Latin translation by Flavio Mitridate survives in Vat. Ebr. 190 as *Liber de secretis orationum et benedictionum*. The section also notes a partially translated anonymous commentary on daily prayers, which Campanini attributes to **Yehudah ben Nissim Ibn Malka**.

### §51 — *Imre sepher, id est eloquia pulchritudinis* (Abraham Abulafia)

The bilingual Hebrew-Latin title leaves little doubt: this is **Abraham Abulafia**'s *Imre Shefer* ("Beautiful Sayings"), which Zorzi renders as "Discorsi di bellezza." Like the *Sefer ha-Temunah*, it was translated by Felice da Prato for Egidio da Viterbo and was intended for publication by Bomberg. Campanini notes that while the *Imre Shefer* is also mentioned in Botarel's Sefer Yetzirah commentary, at least one citation in the *De Harmonia Mundi* appears to derive from Abulafia directly rather than through Botarel, suggesting Zorzi had first-hand access.

## An Uncatalogued Citation: The "Thirty-Two Paths"

Beyond the formal *catalogus*, Campanini notes a further Kabbalistic citation in the *De Harmonia Mundi* (in the fourth tone of the first *Cantico*) where Zorzi writes, without naming an author, about angelic teaching adapted to the capacity of each recipient:

> "Non enim equaliter angeli omnes docent, sed pro captu uniuscuiusque, ut in libro triginta duarum semitarum apud secretiores theologos Hebraeoroum celeberrimo scribitur."

<cite>p. 114 (citing De Harmonia Mundi, Cantico I, tonus IV)</cite>

The reference to the "thirty-two paths" (*triginta duarum semitarum*) points to the Sefer Yetzirah tradition, in which God is said to have created the world through thirty-two paths (the ten *sefirot* plus the twenty-two letters of the Hebrew alphabet). Campanini suggests the specific source may be the *Be'ur ha-L"B netivot* ("Commentary on the Thirty-Two Paths"), a special section within the Sefer Yetzirah commentary attributed to **Avraham ben David** (pseudo-Rabad), integrated into its *Haqdamah* (Preface).

## Campanini's Synthetic Assessment: Zorzi's Place in Renaissance Christian Kabbalah

The chunk closes with Campanini's evaluative conclusion, which has both a cautious and an affirmative dimension. He acknowledges the limitations: many identifications remain uncertain, and Zorzi's dependence on intermediate sources — often unreliable — is undeniable. Yet the richness of first-hand readings is, Campanini argues, "raramente eguagliata nell'intera vicenda della qabbalah cristiana del Rinascimento."

More significantly, Campanini offers an intellectual-historical argument for Zorzi's unique position. Zorzi worked precisely when the Neoplatonic-Neopythagorean synthesis was beginning to fracture into mutually incompatible currents — the new science of **Copernicus, Kepler, and Newton**, the militant Platonism of **Francesco Patrizi**, the speculations of **Giordano Bruno**, and the esotericism of **Athanasius Kircher**. Zorzi, Campanini argues, was perhaps the last thinker to conceive of a unified synthesis in the sign of tradition:

> "Zorzi, lo si è già osservato, forse per l'ultima volta, aveva pensato una possibile sintesi nel segno della tradizione, ma una tradizione quasi del tutto inaudita, in cui il ruolo della qabbalah ebraica, per quanto paradossale ciò possa sembrare, consisteva precisamente nel rendere possibile una lettura cristiana dell'ermetismo che non si riducesse a una semplice platonizzazione del messaggio cristiano."

<cite>p. 116</cite>

This is the interpretive crux of Campanini's entire introduction: Hebrew Kabbalah for Zorzi was not an ornament or a quarry for proof-texts but the structural mechanism that allowed a genuinely Christian reading of Hermetism — one that avoided the reduction of Christianity to mere Platonic philosophy.

The closing image is characteristically musical: the texts and their history are, Campanini writes, "le voci e gli strumenti musicali senza i quali l'armonia del mondo sarebbe condannata a echeggiare nel vuoto" — a phrase that deliberately echoes the title of Zorzi's own masterwork and affirms that philological reconstruction is not merely academic but a participation in the harmony itself.

## Key Technical Terms

- **Sha'are ha-sod ha-yichud we-ha-emunah**: Hebrew, "Chapters on the Secret of Unity and Faith" (El'azar da Worms)
- **Me'irat 'enayim**: Hebrew, "Light of the Eyes" (Isaac of Acre)
- **Keter Shem Tov**: Hebrew, "Crown of the Good Name" (Abraham ben Alexander)
- **Sefer ha-Temunah**: Hebrew, "Book of the Figure"
- **Merkavah / Merkava**: Hebrew, "Chariot" — mystical throne-chariot tradition deriving from Ezekiel 1
- **Heikhalot**: Hebrew, "Palaces" — early Jewish mystical literature
- **Imre Shefer**: Hebrew, "Beautiful Sayings" (Abulafia)
- **Triginta duarum semitarum**: Latin calque for the thirty-two paths of the Sefer Yetzirah
- **Be'ur ha-L"B netivot**: Hebrew, "Commentary on the Thirty-Two Paths"
- **Haqdamah**: Hebrew, "Preface"
